S1: Neurol Clin Pract Neurol Clin Pract 1965 ncp NEURCLINPRACT Neurology: Clinical Practice 2163-0402 2163-0933 American Academy of Neurology PMC11637470 PMC11637470.1 11637470 11637470 39678223 10.1212/CPJ.0000000000200408 CPJ-2024-100139 1 3 178 242 243 Research Article Exercises and Brain Stimulation to Preserve Function in Amyotrophic Lateral Sclerosis A Systematic Review and Meta-Analysis https://orcid.org/0009-0005-2518-2925 Banos Marjorie PT, MSc * https://orcid.org/0009-0006-8647-3694 Preuilh Arnaud PT, MSc * https://orcid.org/0000-0003-1600-413X Pradat Pierre-Francois MD, PhD https://orcid.org/0000-0002-8182-7944 Lackmy-Vallée Alexandra PhD https://orcid.org/0000-0002-0226-7169 Marchand-Pauvert Véronique PhD Sorbonne Université, Inserm, CNRS, Laboratoire d'Imagerie Biomédicale, LIB, F-75006, (MB, AP, P-FP, AL-V, VM-P), Paris; and APHP, Neurology Department in Pitié-Salpêtrière Hospital and ALS Referent Center, F-75013, (P-FP), Paris, France.

S5: The handling editor was Deputy Editor Kathryn Kvam, MD.
S7: 2 2025 11 12 2024 15 1 472507 e200408 22 3 2024 17 9 2024 01 02 2026 01 02 2026 13 12 2024 01 02 2026 © 2024 American Academy of Neurology 2024 American Academy of Neurology Background and Objectives Amyotrophic lateral sclerosis (ALS) is a neurodegenerative disease leading to the loss of motor function and muscle strength.
S8: Nonpharmacologic neuromodulative therapeutic approaches such as active exercise may contribute to preserve motor functions in ALS, but this hypothesis remains debated.
S9: The present meta-analysis first aimed to evaluate the effect of active exercise on function and muscle strength preservation.
S10: Moreover, since the responsiveness to induced neuroplasticity of patients with ALS is being discussed, the second objective was to review the analogous effects of noninvasive brain stimulation (NIBS).
S11: Methods Following PRISMA guidelines, we systematically reviewed PubMed, CENTRAL, NIH PMC, PEDro, ScienceDirect, and Web of Science databases from the period between January 10 and July 1, 2023.
S12: Criteria limited inclusion to randomized controlled trials comparing active exercise (aerobic or resistance) with usual care or NIBS with sham.
S13: The primary outcome was assessed based on functional assessment scores reported on validated clinical scales, and the secondary outcome analysis included muscle strength and neurophysiologic changes.
S14: Methodologic quality of the selected studies was assessed using the Physiotherapy Evidence-Based (PEDro) scale.
S15: Relative risk (RR) and heterogeneity (I 2 ) were calculated with Revman software, and evidence quality was estimated by the GRADE quality scale.
S17: Analysis involved 393 patients among whom 164 underwent active exercise and 155 received usual care, 41 received NIBS and 33 underwent sham stimulations.
S18: The nature of active exercise was consistent across studies but varied in frequency.
S19: NIBS parameters were consistent for stimulation sites and session frequency.
S20: Function was significantly preserved in 5 of 9 studies on active exercise and 2 of 4 NIBS trials.
S21: Meta-analysis on functional scales indicated a moderate quality of evidence for the effectiveness of active exercises (RR = 0.61 [0.18, 1.04] with I 2 = 69%) compared with usual care and very low quality of evidence for NIBS (RR = −1.41 [-0.44, 3.26] with I 2 = 89%).
S22: Only 1 NIBS study revealed neuroplastic changes in the brain.
S23: Discussion Active exercise likely slows functional loss in ALS, but the effects of NIBS need further investigation to support their neuroprotective effectiveness.
S24: Moreover, both interventions require further neurophysiologic investigation to elucidate ALS neuroplasticity.
S25: Trial Registration Information This review has been registered in PROSPERO (CRD42023408121).
